Commercial Mar 27, 2026

Here’s How Much Range The Airbus A350-1000 Has Compared To The Boeing 777-300ER

The Airbus A350-1000 and Boeing 777-300ER are long-haul aviation titans, but the A350's slight edge in range may not be as decisive as it seems.

The A350-1000's marginal range advantage becomes operationally irrelevant given both aircraft's ability to cover virtually all commercial long-haul routes with practical fuel reserves.

See Simple Flying →

eVTOL Mar 27, 2026

AIR claims $1 billion in orders as eVTOL maker pushes personal aircraft

Israel-based electric aircraft developer AIR said it has surpassed $1 billion in orders, driven by orders for its…...

AIR's $1 billion order milestone signals growing investor confidence in eVTOL personal aircraft despite ongoing certification delays industry-wide.

See AeroTime Hub →

Commercial Mar 27, 2026

Here’s How Much Larger The GE9X Is Compared To The GE90

The engine is set to enter commercial service on the upcoming Boeing 777X.

The GE9X's significant size increase over the GE90 addresses the 777X's weight and performance demands, requiring substantial redesign of aircraft systems and ground infrastructure.

See Simple Flying →

How to Assess Safety Risk

A practical walkthrough of safety risk assessment under 14 CFR Part 5.

Part 5 establishes a structured methodology for systematically identifying and evaluating hazards before they manifest as accidents, enabling proactive risk management across operations.

Aerospace Mar 26, 2026

U.S. GSSAP satellites execute GEO handoff to monitor China’s Shijian-29 spacecraft

Commercial space tracking data shows U.S. satellites coordinating maneuvers to maintain proximity and continuous observation of a pair of Chinese spacecraft in geostationary orbit.

Real-time satellite coordination demonstrates how U.S. space surveillance assets are evolving to track potential on-orbit threats in contested GEO environments.

See SpaceNews →

Commercial Mar 26, 2026

8 Hours In A Boeing 737 MAX? Inside United Airlines' New Longest Nonstop Flights

Half of the ten longest routes started in 2025 or will do so in 2026. See them all here!

United's expansion of ultra-long 737 MAX routes signals airlines' confidence in the aircraft's reliability and economics for transcontinental service after certification recovery.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 26, 2026

I'm A Flight Attendant, And These Are The Things I Wish Every Passenger Knew Before Boarding

Practical advice for passengers that makes flights easier.

Flight attendants prioritize safety protocols over passenger comfort preferences, and understanding this hierarchy reduces conflicts and improves cabin operations.

Safety Mar 27, 2026

Safety Culture in Aviation SMS

How organizational culture shapes safety outcomes and what it takes to build a reporting culture that supports SMS.

Organizations with psychologically safe reporting cultures detect hazards earlier through proactive voluntary disclosures, reducing accident precursors before they cascade into incidents.

New Route & 9 More Boosted: JetBlue Keeps Turning Up The Heat In Fort Lauderdale

JetBlue adds Cleveland and boosts nine routes as Fort Lauderdale growth accelerates again.

JetBlue's aggressive Fort Lauderdale expansion signals competitive repositioning against legacy carriers in a major hub market.

Commercial Mar 26, 2026

The Aircraft Set To Replace Delta Air Lines' Iconic Boeing 717

Delta's 717 fleet is soon to be retired, and the airline will be replacing the type's operations with the Airbus A220 family.

Delta's A220 transition consolidates narrow-body operations on a modern, fuel-efficient airframe while retiring aging 717s, reducing fleet complexity and maintenance costs.

Aerospace Mar 26, 2026

Space Command begins phased move to Alabama

The new headquarters building is expected to be completed by 2031...

Space Command's relocation to Alabama signals a significant shift in U.S. defense infrastructure priorities, with aerospace contractors now planning supply chain adjustments around a 2031 operational timeline.

Safety Mar 27, 2026

Surface Safety Spotlight – Los Angeles International Airport, CA (LAX) – From the Flight Deck

Los Angeles International Airport (LAX) is one of the nation’s busiest commercial airports. This short video highlights a runway incursion hazard where pilots exiting Runway 24R, particularly via...

Runway incursion risks at major hubs like LAX persist even with standard procedures, requiring pilots to remain vigilant during high-traffic exit sequences.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Commercial Mar 26, 2026

The 10 Most Popular Transpacific Routes Operated By The Boeing 787 Dreamliner In 2026

The 787 and Transpacific routes really go hand-in-hand. The aircraft provides the capacity and efficiency to make these routes work.

The 787's fuel efficiency and range enable profitable ultra-long-haul transpacific service on routes that would be economically marginal for older wide-body aircraft.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Mar 26, 2026

What’s Up: April 2026 Skywatching Tips from NASA

Mercury shines at its brightest for the year, the Lyrid meteor shower peaks, and a bright new comet makes an appearance in April’s night sky.

April 2026 offers optimal viewing conditions for three celestial events simultaneously, providing rare opportunities for both amateur and professional skywatchers to conduct atmospheric and astronomical observations.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 26, 2026

La NASA presentará el telescopio Roman completo y ofrecerá una conferencia de prensa

Se invita a los medios de comunicación el martes 21 de abril al Centro de Vuelo Espacial Goddard de la NASA en Greenbelt, Maryland, para conocer el telescopio espacial Nancy Grace Roman de la agencia, cuya construcción terminó recientemente y el cual se encuentra ultimando las pruebas previas a su...

NASA's Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope completion marks a significant milestone in space-based infrared astronomy capabilities, with implications for future aerospace technology integration and testing protocols.

See NASA →

Safety Mar 27, 2026

RNO Mustang 9 Departure

Pilots departing from runways 17 left and right at Reno-Tahoe International need to pay special attention to the Mustang 9 Departure. It is extremely important that the procedure on the Mustang 9...

Reno-Tahoe's Mustang 9 Departure requires heightened pilot attention due to specific procedural complexities associated with simultaneous operations from parallel runways 17L/R.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Aerospace Mar 26, 2026

Government use of commercial procurement models has limitations in space

While government agencies are increasingly turning to commercial contracting mechanisms for space capabilities, that does not necessarily translate to additional business opportunities for the companies offering them.

Commercial space contracts with government agencies may not expand company revenues as expected due to structural constraints inherent in government procurement processes.

See SpaceNews →

Commercial Mar 26, 2026

The Simple Flying Podcast Episode 284: Air Canada Crash Latest, United Airlines Introduces Couches In Economy Class

Join Tom & Channing for the latest aviation hot topics.

Air Canada's recent incident and United's premium economy seating represent divergent strategies—safety improvements versus ancillary revenue generation—reflecting competitive pressures reshaping carrier operations.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Mar 26, 2026

NASA to Unveil Complete Roman Telescope, Host Media Briefing

Media are invited Tuesday, April 21, to NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center in Greenbelt, Maryland, for a look at the agency’s Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope, which recently completed construction and is wrapping up prelaunch testing. This will be one of the last opportunities to view the fully...

The Roman Space Telescope's completion and public unveiling marks a critical prelaunch milestone, signaling readiness for deployment of NASA's flagship astrophysics observatory.

See NASA →

Industry Mar 27, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across airlines, airports, and flight planning offers airlines immediate efficiency gains without requiring new aircraft technology, bridging the gap until sustainable fuel and propulsion systems mature.

See MDPI →

Aerospace Mar 26, 2026

NASA Releases Artemis II Moon Mission Launch Countdown

Before NASA sends its astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and CSA (Canadian Space Agency) astronaut Jeremy Hansen on their Artemis II mission around the Moon, the launch team at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida and teams across the country will begin counting down about...

Artemis II's crewed lunar flyby represents a critical validation point for NASA's deep-space exploration architecture before the actual Moon landing attempt in Artemis III.

See NASA →

Business Aviation Mar 26, 2026

Booking Last-Minute Business Class Award Tickets: Everything You Need To Know

Last-minute business class award tickets can offer significant savings relative to purchasing the fares in cash, although this is not guaranteed.

Award availability patterns suggest airlines strategically release premium cabin seats shortly before departure to optimize revenue while maintaining frequent flyer program value perception.

See Simple Flying →

Industry Mar 26, 2026

Navi AI launches publicly with $6 million to expand AI pilot training platform

Navi AI has launched publicly with more than $6 million in funding as it looks to expand its…...

AI-driven pilot training platforms are attracting significant venture capital, signaling industry recognition that simulation-based learning can reduce training costs and accelerate pilot certification timelines.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Mar 26, 2026

Station Nation: Aaron Rose, Cold Stowage Mission Manager

As a member of the Crew and Thermal Systems Division, Aaron Rose supports critical cargo resupply missions to the International Space Station. In this role, he works with payload developers to safely transport temperature-controlled science experiments to and from station with portable coolers,...

ISS payload thermal management requires specialized expertise to preserve experiment integrity during cargo transport, creating niche career opportunities in space logistics.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 26, 2026

Turning growth into profits remains a challenge as space demand grows

While the space economy is expanding across multiple fronts, questions remain about how some of its most promising opportunities could translate growing demand into sustainable profits.

Space industry companies face a profitability gap despite expanding market demand, suggesting venture-backed models may struggle without clearer monetization pathways.

See SpaceNews →

Aerospace Mar 26, 2026

Airbus Tests Multi-Aircraft Aerial Firefighting System

Trial linked aircraft, drones and ground crews for enhanced targeting and coordination.

Airbus's networked approach demonstrates how autonomous coordination between manned aircraft, drones, and ground teams can dramatically improve wildfire response efficiency and safety.

See AVweb →

Safety Mar 26, 2026

Russian YouTuber killed after airplane may have been mistaken for Ukraine drone

Russian aviation blogger Pavel Koshkin was killed when the Alto NG aircraft he was flying crashed near Kolomna,…...

Civilian aircraft operating near conflict zones face heightened risk of misidentification by air defense systems, creating urgent safety implications for general aviation in regional instability.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Mar 26, 2026

Xona raises $170 million for satellite navigation network

The company is scaling up production as it looks to build a 258-satellite network to provide positioning, navigation and timing services...

Xona's $170M funding enables a private alternative to GPS, creating new positioning infrastructure that could reduce aviation's dependence on government-controlled GNSS systems.

See SpaceNews →

General Aviation Mar 26, 2026

Picture of the Day: March 26, 2026

Departing Geneva toward the Matterhorn.

Alpine routing near major European hubs requires precise navigation planning due to terrain-induced wind shear and rapid weather pattern changes in mountainous corridors.

See AVweb →

Safety Mar 26, 2026

“Please Do Something”: Pilots Have Complained About LaGuardia Airport’s Safety For Years

Pilots at LaGuardia Airport have reported multiple near-miss situations due to unclear instructions from air traffic control.

Systemic ATC communication failures at LaGuardia represent a documented operational risk that regulatory agencies have yet to adequately address despite years of pilot reports.

See Simple Flying →

Regulatory Mar 26, 2026

House Committee Advances ALERT Act

Measure includes collision-mitigation requirements, changes for military aircraft operations and limits on some ADS-B data use.

The ALERT Act's collision-mitigation mandate and military operations modifications signal regulatory acceptance of new safety technologies while restricting ADS-B transparency—a pivotal shift balancing safety innovation with operational security.

See AVweb →

International Mar 26, 2026

China sends radar mapping and GNSS augmentation sats into orbit with pair of launches

China conducted a pair of launches in recent days, adding new satellites to the SuperView and CentiSpace low Earth orbit infrastructure constellations.

China's expanding LEO constellation infrastructure enhances precise navigation and Earth observation capabilities, potentially positioning it as a competing alternative to Western GNSS and remote sensing services.

See SpaceNews →

International Mar 26, 2026

Arctic Winter Sea Ice Ties Record Low, NASA, NSIDC Scientists Find

For the second consecutive year, winter sea ice in the Arctic reached a level that matches the lowest peak observed since satellite monitoring began in 1979. On March 15, Arctic sea ice extent reached 5.52 million square miles (14.29 million square kilometers), very close to the 2025 peak of 5.53...

Declining Arctic sea ice extent directly impacts aircraft routing efficiency and operational costs for polar routes increasingly relied upon by international carriers.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 26, 2026

NASA’s IXPE Gets Fresh Look at Supernova

NASA’s IXPE (Imaging X-ray Polarimetry Explorer) mission has taken a new observation of a supernova, RCW 86, seen here in an image released on March 24, 2026. This observation helps fill in a fuller picture of what other telescopes have seen. The full image combines IXPE’s data with legacy...

IXPE's polarimetry data reveals previously undetectable magnetic field structures in supernova remnants, enabling more accurate modeling of particle acceleration mechanisms critical to space weather prediction.

See NASA →

Regulatory Mar 26, 2026

Colorado’s Rocky Mountain Metro Airport Launches Unleaded Avgas

UL94 sales begin with state-backed price support and STC reimbursement.

State-subsidized pricing and STC reimbursement programs create a proven model for accelerating unleaded fuel adoption beyond federal mandates alone.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Mar 26, 2026

Satlantis earnings grow alongside demand for Earth-observation satellites

WASHINGTON – Satlantis reported revenues of 47.8 million euros ($56.4 million) in 2025, with more than 50% of the Spanish company’s income derived from small-satellite sales and operations. Audited earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization were 14.4 million euros. The results...

Earth-observation satellite demand is driving 50%+ revenue growth for small-sat manufacturers, signaling expanding commercial applications beyond traditional aerospace markets.

See SpaceNews →
